items.prompt: A greedy algorithm selects first choice g. To begin an exchange argument, which reference solution should be chosen?
constraints: At least one optimal solution exists.
The goal is to show that an optimal solution can be transformed to begin with g.

options.text: Choose an optimal solution O and locate the earliest position where O differs from the greedy construction.
Use the greedy output as the reference optimum; assuming it is optimal avoids a disagreement.
Choose the worst feasible solution so every exchange appears to improve it.
Choose any example in which the greedy output matches a known optimum.

feedback.reason: An exchange argument must transform an actual optimum. The earliest disagreement isolates one greedy choice while preserving the already matched prefix.
feedback.details:
Start with an optimal solution O, not with the greedy output or an arbitrary feasible solution. If O already matches the greedy sequence, there is nothing to prove at that position. Otherwise identify the first position where O chooses o and the greedy construction chooses g. The common prefix remains fixed, so the proof can focus on replacing o with g under exactly the same prior state.

This setup prevents circular reasoning. Calling the greedy output optimal assumes the conclusion, and improving a poor solution says nothing about preserving optimum value. A single agreeing example also fails to quantify over all legal instances. The first-disagreement pattern transfers to schedules, ordered selections, and constructions where local positions can be compared. It is less direct when solutions are unordered or one change has global coupled effects; then the exchange object must be defined more carefully.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.greedy_output_itself: This assumes the claim being proved. An exchange argument needs an independently optimal reference solution.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.worst_solution: Improving a bad solution does not show that the greedy choice is compatible with an optimum.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_passing_example: An example can illustrate the exchange but cannot establish that every instance admits a no-worse transformation.

items.prompt: An optimal solution O and the greedy construction share a prefix, then O chooses o while greedy chooses g. What is the intended basic exchange?
constraints: The shared prefix must remain unchanged.
The transformed solution must be compared with O under the same objective.

options.text: Replace o with g at the first disagreement, then adjust only the affected local portion needed to restore a valid solution.
Discard the complete suffix of O and copy the unproven greedy suffix.
Swap any two choices in O until g happens to appear earlier.
Append g while retaining o, even if both consume the same constrained resource.

feedback.reason: The exchange should be minimal and analyzable: insert g at the first disagreement and modify only what that replacement requires. Replacing the whole suffix would assume the later greedy choices are already correct.
feedback.details:
A basic exchange isolates one local difference between an optimum and the greedy construction. Replacing o with g preserves the agreed prefix and creates a transformed candidate O′ whose feasibility and objective can be checked directly. Some problems require a small local adjustment—such as removing one conflicting interval or swapping an adjacent inversion—but the transformation should not silently replace the entire unproven remainder.

Minimality matters because each changed component creates another proof obligation. Appending g without removing a conflicting choice can violate feasibility; random swaps have no relation to the greedy property; copying the greedy suffix assumes the induction result before proving it. The transfer rule is to choose an exchange object with bounded effects: one item, one adjacent pair, or one cut-crossing choice. If a local change triggers global interactions, that is a warning that a simple exchange proof may not fit.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.replace_entire_suffix: This imports every later greedy decision without proof and prevents the argument from isolating the first local choice.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.swap_random_pair: An exchange must be connected to the greedy preference and preserve the objective. Arbitrary swaps provide neither guarantee.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.append_g_without_removal: The result may become infeasible or change cardinality. A replacement must account for the constrained resource consumed by o and g.

items.prompt: All jobs are available at time 0. A schedule minimizes maximum lateness, but it contains adjacent jobs i then j with d_i > d_j. What must an exchange argument show about swapping them to j then i?
constraints: Jobs are non-preemptive on one machine.
Lateness is completion time minus deadline.

options.text: The swap does not increase the maximum lateness of i and j, and completion times of all later jobs stay unchanged.
The swap is safe only if j also has shorter processing time than i.
The swap changes the deadlines so the inversion disappears.
Every adjacent swap strictly improves maximum lateness, regardless of deadline order.

feedback.reason: The exchange removes one deadline inversion without worsening the objective. The pair finishes at the same final time after the swap, so later jobs are unchanged, and the earlier deadline is no longer delayed behind the later one.
feedback.details:
Let the pair begin at time t, with processing times p_i and p_j. In either order, the second job completes at t + p_i + p_j, so every job after the pair has the same completion time. When d_j < d_i, moving j first can only reduce j’s completion time. Job i moves later, but its later deadline bounds its lateness relative to the pair’s previous maximum. Thus the maximum lateness contributed by the pair does not increase.

Repeating this exchange removes all deadline inversions and yields nondecreasing deadline order without worsening the optimum. The proof does not require shortest-processing-time order, and deadlines are fixed input data. The transfer boundary is a local swap whose effect on the objective and all later state can be calculated exactly. When pairwise swaps affect global feasibility or rewards, this simple inversion argument may not apply.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.shorter_job_first: Processing time is not the key in the maximum-lateness exchange. The proof compares deadline order and works for arbitrary positive processing times.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.deadlines_become_equal: Deadlines are fixed. The inversion disappears because the jobs are reordered, not because the data changes.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.every_swap_improves: Only swaps that remove the relevant inversion are justified, and the guarantee is no worse rather than always strictly better.

items.prompt: A proof can perform one no-worse exchange that makes an optimal solution agree with the greedy construction at the first disagreement. What justifies repeating the process until the solutions agree?
constraints: Every exchange preserves optimal objective value and feasibility.

options.text: Each exchange strictly advances the first agreement position or decreases a finite inversion count, so the process must terminate.
The greedy implementation is fast, so the proof transformation also terminates correctly.
The process terminates only if the original optimum is unique.
No progress measure is needed because repeatedly applying any exchange eventually works.

feedback.reason: A repeated exchange needs a well-founded progress measure. Moving the first disagreement rightward or reducing inversions proves termination while every intermediate solution remains optimal.
feedback.details:
The proof constructs a sequence of optimal solutions, each closer to the greedy order. A finite measure makes “closer” precise. In a positional construction, the length of the common prefix increases. In a sorting-style proof, the number of inversions decreases. Because the measure cannot improve indefinitely in a finite solution, repeated exchanges terminate at an optimum that follows the greedy order.

Runtime speed does not imply proof termination, and uniqueness is unnecessary—the argument often moves among several different optima. Repetition without a measure can cycle or undo prior work. The transfer rule is to name a quantity that every allowed exchange changes monotonically while feasibility and objective remain invariant. This is also an overlap guard: if one exchange can break earlier agreement, the proposed transformation is not yet a valid basic exchange argument.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.greedy_is_fast: Algorithmic running time and mathematical progress of the exchange transformation are separate claims; a fast implementation does not show that exchanges move monotonically toward agreement.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.optimum_is_unique: Exchange arguments commonly rely on multiple optima. They show that at least one optimum can be transformed into greedy form.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.repeat_without_measure: Without a monotone finite measure, exchanges can cycle, undo earlier agreement, or reintroduce prior inversions, so termination and progress remain unsupported.

items.prompt: Arrange the stages of a basic exchange argument in the canonical logical order.
constraints: Scoring uses preserved adjacent relations, not exact element positions.
The proof aims to transform an optimum into one consistent with the greedy construction.

elements.text: Choose an optimal reference solution O.
Locate the first choice where O differs from the greedy construction.
Replace the conflicting optimal choice with the greedy choice using the smallest required local adjustment.
Prove the transformed solution is feasible and no worse under the objective.
Use a decreasing disagreement measure or induction to repeat on the residual solution.

feedback.reason: The proof first identifies an optimum and the precise disagreement, then defines the exchange, verifies feasibility and objective preservation, and only then repeats or invokes induction.
feedback.details:
Each adjacent relation carries a dependency. An optimal reference is needed before a disagreement can be named. The disagreement identifies the local object to exchange. The transformed solution cannot be used for induction until feasibility and objective preservation have been established. Finally, a progress measure or residual induction explains why repeating the local transformation reaches a greedy-compatible optimum.

Reversing these relations produces common proof defects: exchanging before naming the compared choices makes the transformation ambiguous; repeating before verifying no-worse behavior compounds an unsupported step; starting from the greedy output assumes the conclusion. This template transfers broadly, but the concrete exchange and preserved invariant remain problem-specific. Adjacent-relation scoring reflects those logical dependencies rather than requiring one memorized visual position for every stage.
